# SECOND-KNOWLEDGE-BRAIN.md — Dyslexia-Friendly Learning Material Converter (Skill #233)

*Self-improving knowledge base. Last updated: 2026-06-18. Do not edit manually between crawl runs — use knowledge_updater.py.*

---

## 1. Core Concepts & Frameworks

### 1.1 Phonological Deficit Theory
The dominant neuroscientific explanation for developmental dyslexia. Dyslexic individuals show reduced activation in the left hemisphere phonological processing regions (inferior frontal gyrus, supramarginal gyrus, angular gyrus) and compensatory over-reliance on right-hemisphere and frontal routes. Grapheme-phoneme correspondence rules are acquired inefficiently, leading to slow and inaccurate word decoding. Reading speed and accuracy improve significantly when phonological load is reduced (shorter words, high-frequency vocabulary, chunked text).

**Key citation:** Shaywitz, S.E., & Shaywitz, B.A. (2005). Dyslexia (specific reading disability). *Biological Psychiatry*, 57(11), 1301–1309. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biopsych.2005.01.043

### 1.2 Visual Stress / Meares-Irlen Syndrome
A visual perceptual condition that frequently co-occurs with dyslexia (~20% of dyslexic individuals; Wilkins, 2004). Symptoms include text appearing to move, blur, or vibrate; difficulty tracking lines; headaches and eye strain during reading. Coloured overlays and tinted lenses reduce the pattern glare that triggers visual cortex hyperexcitability. Background colours (cream, light blue, light yellow, mint green) reduce contrast and thereby reduce visual stress for susceptible readers.

**Key citation:** Wilkins, A.J., Sihra, N., & Myers, A. (2005). How precise do precision tints have to be and how many are necessary? *Ophthalmic and Physiological Optics*, 25(3), 269–276. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1475-1313.2005.00288.x

### 1.3 Magnocellular Theory
Proposes a deficit in the magnocellular (large-cell) visual pathway, which processes fast temporal information including saccadic eye movement. Dyslexic readers show slower saccadic return sweeps and shorter fixation durations per word, resulting in more regressions. Wider line spacing (≥ 1.5x) and reduced crowding (larger letter spacing, shorter line length) reduce the demands on the magnocellular system.

**Key citation:** Stein, J., & Walsh, V. (1997). To see but not to read; the magnocellular theory of dyslexia. *Trends in Neurosciences*, 20(4), 147–152. https://doi.org/10.1016/S0166-2236(96)01005-3

### 1.4 Double-Deficit Hypothesis
Wolf & Bowers (1999) proposed that dyslexia results from deficits in phonological awareness, rapid automatised naming (RAN), or both. Double-deficit readers (both deficits) show the most severe reading impairment and require the most comprehensive interventions, including multi-sensory approaches (Orton-Gillingham) alongside typographic accommodations.

**Key citation:** Wolf, M., & Bowers, P.G. (1999). The double-deficit hypothesis for the developmental dyslexias. *Journal of Educational Psychology*, 91(3), 415–438. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-0663.91.3.415

### 1.5 Working Memory Limitations in Dyslexia
Dyslexic readers consistently show reduced phonological working memory capacity (Gathercole & Baddeley, 1993). Long sentences, embedded clauses, and passive constructions increase working-memory load. Sentence length ≤ 15 words and simple SVO structure (subject-verb-object) reduce working-memory demand. Chunking content into small, headed paragraphs externalises the organisational structure that working memory would otherwise need to maintain.

**Key citation:** Gathercole, S.E., & Baddeley, A.D. (1993). *Working Memory and Language*. Erlbaum. ISBN: 978-0-86377-272-2

### 1.6 Crowding Effect
Adjacent letters interfere with each other's recognition in the periphery of visual attention. Increased letter spacing (0.35em additional) reduces crowding, particularly for readers who rely on peripheral vision during saccades. Zorzi et al. (2012) showed letter spacing alone improved reading speed and accuracy in dyslexic children even without font changes.

**Key citation:** Zorzi, M., Barbiero, C., Facoetti, A., Lonciari, I., Carrozzi, M., Montico, M., … & Ziegler, J.C. (2012). Extra-large letter spacing improves reading in dyslexia. *PNAS*, 109(28), 11455–11459. https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1205566109

## 2. Key Research Papers

| # | Title | Authors | Year | Venue | DOI/Link | Relevance |
|---|-------|---------|------|-------|----------|-----------|
| 1 | Extra-large letter spacing improves reading in dyslexia | Zorzi M. et al. | 2012 | PNAS | https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1205566109 | Empirical RCT; core evidence for letter-spacing rule (0.35em) |
| 2 | Effect of font type and size on readability for people with dyslexia | Rello L. & Baeza-Yates R. | 2013 | ACM ASSETS | https://doi.org/10.1145/2513383.2513447 | Eye-tracking RCT; Arial, Helvetica, Verdana, OpenDyslexic vs serif; key font evidence |
| 3 | Dyslexie: A typeface for dyslexics? | Kuster S.M. et al. | 2018 | Dyslexia (Wiley) | https://doi.org/10.1002/dys.1556 | Controlled study of OpenDyslexic/Dyslexie vs Arial; mixed results — Arial equally effective |
| 4 | OpenDyslexic font and reading performance | Wery J.J. & Diliberto J.A. | 2017 | Preventing School Failure | https://doi.org/10.1080/1045988X.2016.1176023 | Classroom study; OpenDyslexic not superior to Arial for all subtypes |
| 5 | The effects of line spacing on the reading of text in normal and dyslexic readers | Reynolds L. | 2003 | J. Research in Reading | https://doi.org/10.1111/1467-9817.00260 | Evidence for 1.5x–2.0x line spacing benefit |
| 6 | Reading with colored overlays | Wilkins A.J. et al. | 2004 | Ophthalmic & Physiological Optics | https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1475-1313.2004.00178.x | Colored overlay RCT; ~20% benefit rate; methodology for preference testing |
| 7 | The double-deficit hypothesis for the developmental dyslexias | Wolf M. & Bowers P.G. | 1999 | J. Educational Psychology | https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-0663.91.3.415 | Foundational classification; phonological + naming speed deficit model |
| 8 | Dyslexia (specific reading disability) | Shaywitz S.E. & Shaywitz B.A. | 2005 | Biological Psychiatry | https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biopsych.2005.01.043 | Neuroimaging evidence; phonological deficit theory overview |
| 9 | To see but not to read; the magnocellular theory of dyslexia | Stein J. & Walsh V. | 1997 | Trends in Neurosciences | https://doi.org/10.1016/S0166-2236(96)01005-3 | Magnocellular pathway deficit; saccadic movement basis for spacing rules |
| 10 | Automatic text simplification to improve readability for dyslexia | Rello L. et al. | 2013 | ACM IUI | https://doi.org/10.1145/2449396.2449444 | NLP-based sentence simplification; confirms ≤ 15-word sentence target |
| 11 | Universal Design for Learning guidelines (UDL 2.2) | CAST | 2018 | cast.org | https://udlguidelines.cast.org | Framework for multiple means of representation; underpins structural aids |
| 12 | Independent review of the teaching of early reading (Rose Report) | Rose J. | 2006 | UK DCSF | https://doi.org/10.1080/02671520701440302 | UK government policy; phonics-first + systematic approach evidence base |
| 13 | Cognitive aspects of reading | Perfetti C. & Stafura J. | 2014 | J. Learning Disabilities | https://doi.org/10.1177/0022219413507062 | Word frequency effects on reading comprehension; supports high-frequency vocabulary substitution |
| 14 | Working memory and literacy: A review | Swanson H.L. & Berninger V. | 1995 | Issues in Education | N/A (review) | Working memory limitations; basis for ≤ 15-word sentence rule |
| 15 | BDA Dyslexia Style Guide | British Dyslexia Association | 2023 | bdadyslexia.org.uk | https://www.bdadyslexia.org.uk/advice/employers/creating-a-dyslexia-friendly-workplace/dyslexia-friendly-style-guide | Primary typography standard; all numeric targets sourced here |

## 3. State-of-the-Art Methods & Tools

### 3.1 Assistive Reading Technology
| Tool | Type | Key Feature | Limitation |
|------|------|-------------|------------|
| ReadSpeaker | TTS/accessibility | Text-to-speech with dyslexia-specific voice rates; highlights text as read | Requires internet; licence cost |
| Kurzweil 3000 | Educational AT | OCR + TTS + vocabulary support + colour highlighting | Windows/Mac only; expensive |
| ClaroRead | Accessibility suite | Word prediction, TTS, colour overlays, OpenDyslexic font switch | Subscription model |
| Immersive Reader (Microsoft) | Built-in AT | Line focus, syllabification, picture dictionary, TTS | Microsoft ecosystem only |
| Natural Reader | TTS | Free tier available; multiple languages | Limited formatting conversion |
| Beeline Reader | Colour-gradient reading | Progressive colour gradient reduces line-following errors | Web/extension only |

### 3.2 AI-Powered Text Simplification
| Method | Description | Best Evidence |
|--------|-------------|---------------|
| Lexical substitution | Replace low-frequency with high-frequency synonyms (WordNet, PPDB) | Rello et al. (2013) — 8% reading speed improvement |
| Sentence splitting | Automatic splitting at syntactic boundaries (dependency parse) | Siddharthan (2014) — ARI reduction without meaning loss |
| Syntactic simplification | Remove embedded clauses, reduce passive voice (OpenNMT/T5-based) | Niklaus et al. (2019) DISSIM dataset |
| Readability-controlled generation | LLM (GPT-4, Claude) with Flesch-Kincaid as a scoring constraint | Martin et al. (2022) MUSS (multilingual) |

### 3.3 Font Research Summary
Based on Rello & Baeza-Yates (2013) eye-tracking study (n=48, dyslexic + control):
- **Best performing:** Helvetica, Courier, Arial, Verdana — comparable performance
- **OpenDyslexic:** No statistically significant advantage over Arial in most studies (Kuster et al., 2018; Wery & Diliberto, 2017)
- **Worst performing:** Times New Roman, Garamond (serif fonts)
- **Practical recommendation:** Arial or Verdana (universally available) as default; OpenDyslexic for users who report subjective benefit

## 5. Analytical Frameworks

### 5.1 BDA Dyslexia Style Guide (2023) — Primary Standard
The British Dyslexia Association's Good Practice Guide provides the most comprehensive and widely adopted typography standards for dyslexia accessibility. All numeric targets in this skill derive from the 2023 edition.

**Mandatory requirements (BDA 2023):**
- Font: Arial, Verdana, Tahoma, Century Gothic, Trebuchet, Calibri, or OpenDyslexic
- Avoid: Times New Roman, Arial Narrow, any decorative/italic font as body text
- Size: minimum 12pt; 14pt for children; 16pt for severe cases
- Line spacing: 1.5x minimum (paragraph spacing ≥ 2x)
- Letter spacing: 0.35em additional beyond font default
- Alignment: left-aligned (ragged right) — never justified, centred, or right-aligned for body text
- Column width: 60–70 characters maximum
- Contrast: sufficient contrast (WCAG 4.5:1 minimum); avoid pure black on white
- Background: cream, light yellow, or light blue preferred over pure white
- No ALL-CAPS for sentences (uppercase reduces word-shape recognition)
- No underline for emphasis (reserve for hyperlinks); use bold instead
- Sentence length: ≤ 20 words recommended; ≤ 15 words for primary-age learners

### 5.2 IDA Standards
The International Dyslexia Association's Fact Sheets and position papers align with BDA on font and spacing. Additionally emphasise:
- Orton-Gillingham multi-sensory approach for instruction (visual + auditory + kinaesthetic)
- Structured literacy principles for text organisation
- Importance of comprehension supports (glossaries, headers, summaries)

### 5.3 WCAG 2.1 / 2.2 — Text Presentation
Relevant Success Criteria:
- **SC 1.4.3 Contrast (Minimum) — Level AA:** Text colour vs background ≥ 4.5:1 (normal text); ≥ 3:1 (large text ≥ 18pt or 14pt bold)
- **SC 1.4.4 Resize text — Level AA:** Text can be resized up to 200% without loss of content or functionality
- **SC 1.4.12 Text Spacing — Level AA:** Line height ≥ 1.5x font size; letter spacing ≥ 0.12em; word spacing ≥ 0.16em; paragraph spacing ≥ 2x font size — no loss of content or functionality

### 5.4 Universal Design for Learning (UDL) Framework — CAST 2018
Three primary UDL principles relevant to dyslexia conversion:
1. **Multiple Means of Representation (Principle 1):** Offer text in multiple formats (audio, visual aids, glossaries). Relevant sub-guidelines: 1.1 (alternatives for text), 1.3 (customise display), 2.1 (clarify vocabulary), 3.1 (activate background knowledge).
2. **Multiple Means of Action & Expression (Principle 2):** Support planning and strategy development.
3. **Multiple Means of Engagement (Principle 3):** Optimise individual choice and autonomy (e.g., font/colour preference).

### 5.5 Orton-Gillingham Approach
Multi-sensory structured language instruction (phonics-based). Relevant to this skill's sentence simplification: OG recommends systematic, cumulative, sequential text presentation — short sentences building to longer; explicit vocabulary scaffolding; no assumed background knowledge.

### 5.6 Flesch-Kincaid Readability Metrics
Two complementary formulas used throughout this skill:

**Flesch-Kincaid Reading Ease (FKRE):**
```
FKRE = 206.835 − (1.015 × ASL) − (84.6 × ASW)
```
Where ASL = average sentence length in words, ASW = average syllables per word.
- Score 90–100: Very easy (age ~11)
- Score 60–70: Standard (age ~13–15) — minimum target for this skill
- Score 30–50: Difficult (college level)
- Score 0–30: Very difficult (professional)

**Flesch-Kincaid Grade Level (FKGL):**
```
FKGL = (0.39 × ASL) + (11.8 × ASW) − 15.59
```
Target: ≤ 8 for general adult dyslexic audience; ≤ 5 for primary-age learners.

## 8. Cross-Skill References

This skill is part of the **career-education** cluster. Reusable components and natural pairings:

| Skill / Component | Reuse opportunity |
|--------------------|--------------------|
| `sub-profile-intake` | Generalise for any learner cohort (ADHD, ESL, low-vision, mature learners) |
| `sub-readability-scorer` | Extract as `sub-scoring-engine` for accessibility auditing skills |
| `sub-font-layout-converter` | Reuse for document-accessibility skills (visual impairment, PDF remediation) |
| CV / resume skills | Apply BDA font/spacing rules to job-application materials |
| E-learning script skills | Use sentence-simplification engine before LMS upload |
| Interview-prep skills | Convert dense legal/policy passages using the legal-text adjusted threshold |

## 6. Self-Update Protocol

### Crawl Configuration (tools/knowledge_updater.py)

```yaml
sources:
  - name: PubMed
    base_url: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/
    api: https://eutils.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/entrez/eutils/esearch.fcgi
    search_terms:
      - "dyslexia font readability"
      - "dyslexia letter spacing"
      - "visual stress dyslexia overlay"
      - "OpenDyslexic effectiveness"
      - "text simplification dyslexia"
      - "Universal Design for Learning reading"
    fields: title, authors, year, journal, doi, abstract
    max_results_per_query: 20

  - name: Dyslexia (Wiley)
    base_url: https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/10990909
    method: WileyCrawler (requests + optional crawl4ai)
    search_terms: ["dyslexia", "reading disability", "visual stress"]

  - name: Annals of Dyslexia (Springer)
    base_url: https://link.springer.com/journal/11881
    method: SpringerCrawler (requests + optional crawl4ai)
    search_terms: ["dyslexia", "phonological dyslexia", "reading intervention"]

  - name: Journal of Learning Disabilities (Sage)
    base_url: https://journals.sagepub.com/home/ldx
    method: SageCrawler (requests + optional crawl4ai)
    search_terms: ["dyslexia", "learning disabilities reading", "text accessibility"]

  - name: ArXiv cs.CL
    base_url: https://arxiv.org/search/
    search_terms:
      - "text simplification accessibility"
      - "readability dyslexia NLP"
    fields: title, authors, year, arxiv_id, abstract

scoring:
  recency_weight: 0.5   # 1.0 = published today; decays by 1/365 per day
  relevance_weight: 0.5 # keyword match count / total keywords
  min_score: 0.3        # discard entries below this threshold

deduplication:
  method: sha256_hash
  fields: [doi, url]   # hash of DOI if present, else URL

append_format: |
  | {id} | {title} | {authors_short} | {year} | {journal} | {doi_link} | {relevance_note} |

schedule: weekly (Sunday 02:00 UTC)
```

### Running the Updater
```bash
cd D:\Dungchan\skill_adv\233\tools
python knowledge_updater.py --brain ../SECOND-KNOWLEDGE-BRAIN.md --max-entries 20
```
